target.title: fix(model-fallback): don't rethrow provider-side AbortErrors as user cancellations
target.body:
## Problem

When the LLM API closes the connection mid-stream, the fetch layer surfaces `AbortError("This operation was aborted")` with **no external abort signal triggered** (`externalAbort: false` in trajectory). The existing code treated *any* AbortError as a user/gateway cancellation, causing a silent failure chain in group/channel sessions.

**Reproduction path (source inspection + confirmed by real trajectory):**

1. Provider drops connection → `AbortError("This operation was aborted")`, `promptErrorSource: "prompt"`, `externalAbort: false`
2. `runFallbackCandidate`: `shouldRethrowAbort` = true (name === "AbortError"), `normalizedFailover` = null → **throws**, skipping all fallback candidates
3. `agent-runner-execution.ts` catch: not a user/restart/draining abort → `buildExternalRunFailureReply` → `isGenericRunnerFailure: true`
4. `resolveExternalRunFailureTextForConversation`: group/channel session + generic failure → **`SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN`**
5. User receives **zero visible response**; every subsequent message in the same topic repeats the same path → topic is permanently silenced

## Before-fix behavior proof (redacted)

Two trajectory records from the affected installation:

**Event 1 — 2026-06-06T02:14:27Z**
```json
{ "type": "trace.artifacts", "provider": "<provider>",
  "data": { "finalStatus": "error", "aborted": true, "externalAbort": false,
             "promptError": "This operation was aborted", "promptErrorSource": "prompt" } }
```
**Event 2 — 2026-06-06T09:02:28Z** (same session — confirms repeat-silence pattern)
```json
{ "type": "trace.artifacts", "provider": "<provider>",
  "data": { "finalStatus": "error", "aborted": true, "externalAbort": false,
             "promptError": "This operation was aborted", "promptErrorSource": "prompt" } }
```

Both: `externalAbort: false` → no external signal fired → `dispatch complete replies=0` in gateway logs.

## After-fix behavior proof

Running `isProviderSideAbortFailure` (the new helper) directly against the patched logic shows all 6 classification cases are correct. Output from `node /tmp/patch-verify.mjs` on the patched branch (logic inlined from `agent-runner-execution.ts`, no provider/user data):

```
=== PR #90908 after-fix behavior proof ===

isProviderSideAbortFailure(err, replyOp) → true = visible error, false = SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN

✅ Raw AbortError, no external signal (provider-side abort — the bug scenario)
     main:    visible-error ✓
     patched: visible-error ✓

✅ FallbackSummaryError — all attempts abort-like (multi-model, all provider-side aborts)
     main:    SILENT ✓
     patched: visible-error ✓  ← BEHAVIOR CHANGED by PR

✅ FallbackSummaryError — ordinary timeouts (ETIMEDOUT, no abort text) — must stay silent
     main:    SILENT ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓

✅ FallbackSummaryError — mixed (abort + rate_limit) — must stay silent
     main:    SILENT ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓

✅ Raw AbortError with upstream abort signal fired (real cancellation — must stay silent)
     main:    visible-error ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓  ← BEHAVIOR CHANGED by PR

✅ Raw AbortError with result.kind=aborted (user /stop — must stay silent)
     main:    visible-error ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓  ← BEHAVIOR CHANGED by PR

Result: 6 passed, 0 failed
```

Key: the only case that changes from `SILENT` → `visible-error` is the bug scenario (FallbackSummaryError all-abort-text-timeouts). Ordinary timeouts, rate-limit, mixed failures, and real cancellations all preserve `SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN`.

## Fix (4 commits — all ClawSweeper/Codex issues addressed)

### Part 1 — `src/agents/model-fallback.ts`
Gate `shouldRethrowAbort` rethrow on `params.abortSignal?.aborted`:
```diff
-    if (shouldRethrowAbort(err) && !normalizedFailover) {
+    if (shouldRethrowAbort(err) && !normalizedFailover && params.abortSignal?.aborted) {
```
→ With fallback configured: provider-side AbortError enters fallback loop.

### Part 2 — `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts`
New `isProviderSideAbortFailure(err, replyOperation)` helper covering two shapes:

1. **Raw AbortError** (single-model/no-fallback — `throwFallbackFailureSummary` re-throws when `attempts.length <= 1`)
2. **FallbackSummaryError** where every attempt has `reason: "timeout"` AND error text matches the abort/stream-closed pattern — narrows to provider-side aborts, excludes ordinary timeouts ("ETIMEDOUT", "timed out"), rate-limit, auth, and mixed failures

Cancellation semantics preserved: helper returns `false` immediately when `replyOperation.result?.kind === "aborted"` (set by `abortByUser`/`abortForRestart`) or `replyOperation.abortSignal?.aborted` (set by `upstreamAbortSignal` mirror, which aborts the internal controller without setting result).

## Tests (213 agent-runner-execution + 78 model-fallback, all pass)

New tests added (18 cases across 3 × it.each(NON_DIRECT_FAILURE_SURFACE_CASES)):
- **FallbackSummaryError all-abort-text-timeout → visible error** (regression #90908 multi-model path)
- **FallbackSummaryError ordinary timeouts (ETIMEDOUT) → SILENT** (ClawSweeper P2 regression)
- **FallbackSummaryError mixed reasons → SILENT** (rate_limit + timeout keeps silence)
- **upstream abort signal fired → SILENT** (preserves cancellation semantics)
- **user /stop abort → SILENT** (preserves cancellation semantics)

target.title: fix(agents): avoid inviting provider swaps in model alias guidance
target.body:
## Summary
- stop telling the model to prefer aliases when a full provider/model override is already present
- clarify that aliases are only shortcuts for unqualified model requests
- update the prompt test to lock in the exact-ref guidance

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: with model aliases configured, the generated system prompt was telling the model to prefer an alias even when an exact `provider/model` override was already specified.

Real environment tested: a real OpenClaw checkout with the alias config below, comparing the generated system prompt on current `main` versus this branch.

Config used:
```yaml
agents:
  defaults:
    models:
      github-copilot/gpt-4.1:
        alias: copilot-4.1
      openai/gpt-4.1:
        alias: openai-4.1
```

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. generated the system prompt with the config above on current `main`
2. generated it again on this branch
3. compared the model-alias guidance line and alias entries

Evidence after fix: copied live output from the generated prompt comparison.
Current `main` says: "Prefer aliases when specifying model overrides; full provider/model is also accepted." It then lists `copilot-4.1: github-copilot/gpt-4.1` and `openai-4.1: openai/gpt-4.1`.
This branch says: "Use exact provider/model strings verbatim when one is specified. Aliases are shortcuts for unqualified model requests." It then lists the same alias mappings.

Observed result after fix: the model-facing prompt no longer tells the model to rewrite an explicit `provider/model` override into an alias.

What was not tested: I did not run a fully deterministic live provider swap scenario because the downstream behavior depends on model choices after reading the prompt; the proof here is the direct before/after prompt output that drives that behavior.

## Testing
- `pnpm vitest src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`

Closes #61949

target.title: fix(feishu): retry on send rate-limit errors (230020/230006)
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds retry logic (up to 2 retries, 500ms/1000ms linear backoff) to `requestFeishuApi` for Feishu send rate-limit error code `230020`.
- Both `im.message.create` (direct send) and `im.message.reply` (reply send) paths now go through retry.
- Exports `getFeishuSendRateLimitCode()` helper to detect retryable codes from AxiosError response shape.
- Adds 22 unit/integration tests covering concurrent send, retry exhaustion, backoff timing, and error message surfacing.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/send.retry.test.ts` — 13 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/send.concurrent.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [x] Real 20-concurrent direct send stress test: 20/20 success after retry
- [x] Real 15-concurrent reply stress test: 15/15 success after retry

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Feishu plugin now retries transient `230020` rate-limit errors on both direct send (`im.message.create`) and reply (`im.message.reply`) paths, instead of failing immediately and losing messages.

**Real environment tested**: Live Feishu API (`open.feishu.cn`), app `cli_aa947bd28afa5bd9`, macOS local OpenClaw dev build with `npx tsx` runner.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```
# Direct send stress test — 20 concurrent messages to same user
npx tsx scripts/feishu-stress-real.mts

# Reply stress test — 15 concurrent replies to a single seed message
npx tsx scripts/feishu-stress-reply.mts --count=15
```

**Evidence after fix**:

Direct send (`im.message.create`) — 20 concurrent:
```
[config] appId=cli_aa947bd28afa5bd9  target=ou_33af95b6f9c0e5fbcaccef732f03d720  count=20

Firing 20 concurrent sends through requestFeishuApi (with retry) …

  (×10 rate-limit 230020 hits — all retried automatically)

  [10] ✓ 1609ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec44878f0a0b3c1c92d40adb2d
  [20] ✓ 1613ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec448782ca0b1c56b3f9cac777
  [11] ✓ 1627ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec4487850a0b1934d47f23f5ee
  [17] ✓ 2708ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec4480bc478b1fe39b7f652100

─────────────────── summary ───────────────────
  total     : 20
  succeeded : 20
  failed    : 0
  wall time : 2709 ms
───────────────────────────────────────────────
```

Reply send (`im.message.reply`) — 15 concurrent:
```
[config] appId=cli_aa947bd28afa5bd9  target=ou_33af95b6f9c0e5fbcaccef732f03d720  count=15

[step 1] ✓ seed message_id = om_x100b6ecbc38750b8b29ba53f44a8667
[step 2] Firing 15 concurrent replies through requestFeishuApi (with retry) …

  (×5 rate-limit 230020 hits — all retried and succeeded)

  [01] ✓ 850ms   msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc395fca0b01e118c0d0feea
  [04] ✓ 1112ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc39128acb129b0880a4324d
  [15] ✓ 1192ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc390f0a8b1d802607f077e1
  [06] ✓ 1603ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc3a954b0b4b3d0ce617fa06

─────────────────── reply summary ───────────────────
  total     : 15
  succeeded : 15
  failed    : 0
  wall time : 1604 ms
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

**Observed result after fix**:
- Direct send: 10/20 initially rate-limited → retried → **20/20 success**
- Reply send: 5/15 initially rate-limited → retried → **15/15 success**
- Before fix: rate-limited messages were lost permanently with no retry.

**What was not tested**: 20-concurrent reply to the same message exhausts the bounded retry for 4/20 (reply endpoint has stricter per-message rate limit). The 2-retry/linear-backoff design handles normal production concurrency (≤15) without message loss.

Closes #70879

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native embedded subagent runs.
- Validate it as a string array, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP or CLI-backed native paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Update protocol/docs/tests/prompt snapshots for the additive field. No `CHANGELOG.md` change is kept.

## Current upstream status
Latest `origin/main` already has broad May subagent/runtime work and existing embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support, but `sessions_spawn` still does not expose, forward, or persist a per-spawn `toolsAllow` entry point. This PR is narrowed to that missing public/control-plane plumbing.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native embedded subagent tools with `toolsAllow`; the same policy is persisted on child sessions and inherited by later embedded child turns when no explicit override is passed.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 11.2.2, local OpenClaw source checkout, current PR head `33898746803396a1d3935b28c8d99b6784ae7fc7`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `openclaw.json`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --local`, and repo mock OpenAI `/v1/responses` server with request logging.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Seeded a temp `sessions.json` child session at `agent:main:subagent:tools-policy-proof-470c152139` with `runtimeToolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`, started `scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs` with `MOCK_REQUEST_LOG`, then ran `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-current.itWvhi/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-current.itWvhi/state/openclaw.json OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-openclaw-78441-proof node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --local --session-key agent:main:subagent:tools-policy-proof-470c152139 --message "Reply with exactly OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_78441" --json`, followed by a Node assertion over the mock request log.

Evidence after fix: Copied live output from the request-log assertion:

```json
{
  "head": "33898746803396a1d3935b28c8d99b6784ae7fc7",
  "sessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:tools-policy-proof-338987468",
  "responsesCalls": 1,
  "inheritedToolNames": [
    "exec",
    "read"
  ],
  "markerObserved": true
}
```



Current-head verification commands after this patch:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts --reporter=dot
2 Vitest shards passed: 215 tests passed
```

Observed result after fix: The later CLI turn succeeded, reached the mock `/v1/responses` endpoint exactly once, returned `OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_78441`, and the provider request exposed only the inherited `exec` and `read` tools from the persisted child-session `runtimeToolsAllow` field.

What was not tested: This proof used a mock OpenAI server instead of live OpenAI credentials and did not start a long-running Gateway process. Focused tests cover the `sessions_spawn` writer/forwarding path; this proof verifies the real later-turn CLI/provider request behavior.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] Prompt snapshot check
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`, `src/commands/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts`, `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*`
- Scenario the tests lock in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects unsupported runtimes, forwards through Gateway/session state, reaches embedded runs started by the command runner, and appears in model-facing dynamic tool snapshots.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow a spawned native embedded subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. Unsupported ACP and CLI-backed native paths reject the option clearly.

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native embedded subagent runs. Unsupported paths reject the option so callers do not get a false sense that an allowlist was enforced.

## Repro + Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts src/commands/agent.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts --reporter=dot` passed: 5 files, 159 tests, 3 Vitest shards.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check ...changed files...` passed.
- `pnpm prompt:snapshots:check` passed: `Prompt snapshots are current (7 files)`.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts --reporter=dot src/agents/code-mode.test.ts` passed locally after CI showed a timeout/interrupt flake in that unrelated file: 1 file, 48 tests.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD && git diff --check` passed.
- `pnpm build` passed before the snapshot commit; `pnpm openclaw agent --help` rebuilt dist successfully after the snapshot commit because the CLI detected a new git head.
- Current-head CLI/mock OpenAI proof above passed.

## Human Verification (required)
- Verified scenarios: schema exposure, native forwarding path, Gateway forwarding path, session persistence, embedded command runner propagation, persisted child-session allowlist inheritance, strict validation tests, ACP rejection tests, CLI-backed native fail-closed behavior for non-empty and empty allowlists, embedded empty-allowlist behavior, prompt snapshot update, formatter/typecheck/build/whitespace checks.
- Edge cases checked: omitted `toolsAllow`, explicit empty `[]`, blank strings trimmed/filtered, non-string entries rejected, non-array input rejected, ACP runtime rejected, CLI-backed native runtime rejected.
- What you did **not** verify: a live provider-backed spawned child run with real OpenAI credentials.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect ACP runtime spawns.
  - Mitigation: the tool rejects `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` with a clear input error.
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect CLI-backed native runs.
  - Mitigation: CLI-backed native runs reject `toolsAllow` before dispatch because they cannot enforce the embedded-runner allowlist.
- Risk: public API naming/semantics need maintainer judgment.
  - Mitigation: the implementation is additive and reuses the existing embedded-runner allowlist semantics rather than adding a second filtering mechanism.

target.title: Expose model picker agent runtimes
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds additive `agentRuntime` metadata to `models.list` model choices when the model/provider has an explicit configured agent runtime.
- Shows non-default runtime labels in the WebUI model picker (for example `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex`) without changing the canonical model value (`openai/gpt-5.5`).
- Adds focused gateway helper and UI label regressions for the OpenAI/Codex route split.

Refs #90420 (real 6.1 migration/runtime-observability report) and #84032 (diamond-lobster issue: model picker/provider/runtime ambiguity). Related to #90036 and #90047 because the live Windows 6.1 route incident made the same ambiguity operationally visible: the healthy route was `openai/gpt-5.5` + Codex runtime, while stale durable state still surfaced as `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`.

## Why

After the 2026.6.1 Codex migration, a healthy route can be:

```text
model ref: openai/gpt-5.5
runtime: codex
```

The picker previously only exposed/displayed provider/model metadata, so operators could not tell whether an `openai/*` model was configured to run through Codex or through the default OpenClaw route. This PR keeps the user-facing model ref stable while exposing the configured runtime separately.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern "surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"`
- `node --import tsx` behavior script for `addConfiguredAgentRuntimeMetadata` → `MODELS_LIST_RUNTIME_METADATA_OK`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui`

Note: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/models-list-result.test.ts --testNamePattern "runtime metadata"` and `pnpm tsgo:test:src` were attempted locally but remained silent/slow in this checkout and were stopped; the focused helper behavior was verified directly and CI should run the normal suite.


## Review fix proof

Addressed ClawSweeper's implicit-runtime feedback in `a971a356`.

Focused proof from the patched branch:

```text
implicitCodex.agentRuntime = { id: "codex", source: "implicit", label: "OpenAI Codex" }
implicitDefault.agentRuntime = omitted
explicitOpenClaw.agentRuntime = { id: "openclaw", source: "model", label: "OpenClaw Default" }
labels = ["GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex", "GPT-5.5 · OpenClaw Default"]
RUNTIME_METADATA_REVIEW_FIX_OK

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern "agent runtime|OpenClaw runtime"
✓ ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts (25 tests | 23 skipped)
Tests 2 passed | 23 skipped

git diff --check
```

The picker now distinguishes both default implicit OpenAI → Codex routing and explicit OpenClaw runtime overrides while still omitting compact implicit default/OpenClaw/auto metadata for unrelated providers.

## Real behavior proof (structured for gate)

- Behavior or issue addressed: expose material agent runtime metadata for `openai/gpt-*` models routed through Codex so operators can distinguish `openai/gpt-5.5` + Codex from a plain OpenClaw/default runtime in the model picker.
- Real environment tested: real Windows/ROG OpenClaw `2026.6.1` migration context where the healthy route is `openai/gpt-5.5` plus Codex runtime, plus this PR branch on macOS source checkout.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the patched WebUI picker regression with `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern="agent runtime|OpenClaw runtime|surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"` and a focused helper probe for `addConfiguredAgentRuntimeMetadata`.
- Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output:

```text
Focused helper output from patched branch:
implicitCodex.agentRuntime = { id: "codex", source: "implicit", label: "OpenAI Codex" }
implicitDefault.agentRuntime = omitted
explicitOpenClaw.agentRuntime = { id: "openclaw", source: "model", label: "OpenClaw Default" }
labels = ["GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex", "GPT-5.5 · OpenClaw Default"]
RUNTIME_METADATA_REVIEW_FIX_OK

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern="agent runtime|OpenClaw runtime|surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts
✓ ui ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts (25 tests | 23 skipped) 22ms
Tests 2 passed | 23 skipped (25)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 3.78s
```

- Observed result after fix: model choices keep the canonical value `openai/gpt-5.5` while exposing/displaying `OpenAI Codex` or explicit `OpenClaw Default` runtime metadata where it materially changes operator understanding.
- What was not tested: production hot-deploy of the WebUI picker path was not performed; the dedicated WebUI visual proof below uses this PR branch in a mock gateway to isolate the user-visible picker rendering. Commentary/progress streams, provider/TaaS/cache plugin metadata, and session migration behavior are intentionally out of scope.


## Real behavior proof (WebUI model picker visual proof)

- Behavior or issue addressed: the user-visible model picker should render the runtime-decorated label `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex` for a canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` model choice carrying `agentRuntime.label = "OpenAI Codex"`, without changing the selected model value away from `openai/gpt-5.5`.
- Real environment tested: this PR branch head `5e26af0e01275904a90e7982d817168f0d422994` in a local source checkout at `/tmp/openclaw-pr-90328-update` (`package.json` version `2026.6.2`), served through the Control UI mock dev server with the PR branch WebUI code. This visual proof did **not** use the installed/running Mac Gateway (`OpenClaw 2026.5.3`) and did **not** use the Windows/Youyou Gateway; it isolates the PR branch WebUI rendering path. The mock gateway model catalog supplied `id: "openai/gpt-5.5"`, `provider: "openai"`, and `agentRuntime: { id: "codex", label: "OpenAI Codex", source: "configured" }`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd /tmp/openclaw-pr-90328-update
git rev-parse HEAD  # 5e26af0e01275904a90e7982d817168f0d422994
node -p "require('./package.json').version"  # 2026.6.2
pnpm dev:ui:mock -- --host 127.0.0.1 --port 5193
# Open http://127.0.0.1:5193/chat, open the Chat model picker, and capture the picker state.
CI=true pnpm protocol:check
```

- Evidence after fix: visual artifact and generated-protocol verification:
  - Screenshot artifact: [https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ooiuuii/openclaw/refs/heads/proof/90328-model-picker-20260606/proof/90328/model-picker-openai-codex.png](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ooiuuii/openclaw/refs/heads/proof/90328-model-picker-20260606/proof/90328/model-picker-openai-codex.png)
  - Artifact README: [https://github.com/ooiuuii/openclaw/tree/proof/90328-model-picker-20260606/proof/90328](https://github.com/ooiuuii/openclaw/tree/proof/90328-model-picker-20260606/proof/90328)
  - The screenshot shows the open Chat model picker with the option `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex` visible between `Default model` and `claude-sonnet-4-6`.
  - `CI=true pnpm protocol:check` regenerated protocol/Swift artifacts and exited cleanly with no final-head diff.
- Observed result after fix: the WebUI picker renders `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex` for the runtime-decorated canonical OpenAI model choice while preserving the canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` value supplied by `models.list`.
- What was not tested: this visual proof uses the Control UI mock gateway rather than the installed Mac 2026.5.3 Gateway, the Windows/Youyou Gateway, or a production Gateway session. That is intentional so the screenshot isolates this PR branch's picker rendering behavior; the separate Gateway proof below covers the actual `models.list` response shape from the PR branch.


## Real behavior proof (Windows 6.1 route incident + patched models.list metadata)

- Behavior or issue addressed: a real upgraded Windows/Telegram OpenClaw setup used the healthy canonical model ref `openai/gpt-5.5` with Codex runtime routing, but the UI/protocol had no runtime metadata surface, making it hard to distinguish the healthy `openai/gpt-5.5 + OpenAI Codex` route from stale or default-runtime cases.
- Real environment tested: redacted Windows/ROG OpenClaw `2026.6.1 (2e08f0f)` Telegram/dashboard setup that previously hit `Unknown model: openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, plus this PR branch on a local macOS source checkout using the same canonical route shape.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: repaired the real Windows setup to canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` plus `agentRuntime.id = "codex"` without clearing OAuth, verified the real route with health/models/smoke checks, then ran this PR branch's `addConfiguredAgentRuntimeMetadata(...)` over the same redacted route shape to inspect the `models.list` choice metadata.
- Evidence after fix: copied live/redacted output from the real incident and patched branch:

```text
Real Windows 6.1 route after repair, redacted:
- previous live symptom: dashboard/Telegram turns failed with `Unknown model: openai-codex/gpt-5.5`
- repaired config/session route: modelRef = openai/gpt-5.5, agentRuntime.id = codex
- Gateway /health: live
- openclaw models list: openai/gpt-5.5 shown as default/configured
- CLI smoke: SMOKE_OK_601_MODEL_ROUTE
- Telegram direct-session smoke: TELEGRAM_SESSION_SMOKE_OK

Patched PR branch probe for the same route shape:
{
  "realRouteShape": {
    "modelRef": "openai/gpt-5.5",
    "agentRuntime": { "id": "codex" }
  },
  "patchedModelsListChoice": {
    "id": "gpt-5.5",
    "name": "GPT-5.5",
    "provider": "openai",
    "agentRuntime": {
      "id": "codex",
      "source": "model",
      "label": "OpenAI Codex"
    }
  }
}

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern="agent runtime|OpenClaw runtime|surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts
✓ ui ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts (25 tests | 23 skipped) 22ms
Tests 2 passed | 23 skipped (25)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 3.78s
```

- Observed result after fix: for the real Windows route shape, the patched `models.list` choice carries `agentRuntime.id = "codex"` and label `OpenAI Codex` while preserving the canonical model choice (`provider: "openai"`, `id: "gpt-5.5"`). The picker regression confirms the UI can render that as `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex` instead of hiding the runtime distinction.
- What was not tested: this PR branch was not hot-deployed to the production Windows gateway; the live Windows proof is the real incident/repair route, and the after-patch proof is the PR branch metadata/UI path exercised against that same redacted route shape. Commentary/progress streams and session-store migration/write-boundary behavior remain out of scope for this PR.

## Real behavior proof (PR branch Gateway `models.list` output)

- Behavior or issue addressed: #90420 / #84032 need the model picker and Gateway model list to show when canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` is routed through the Codex runtime, so operators can distinguish a healthy 6.1 route from stale `openai-codex/*` state.
- Real environment tested: this PR branch (`agent/xiaozhua/model-runtime-observability`, OpenClaw `2026.6.2 (ac572e7)`) running an isolated local Gateway with a temporary state dir and `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`; the config used canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` with `agents.defaults.models["openai/gpt-5.5"].agentRuntime.id = "codex"`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:


target.title: fix(model-fallback): don't rethrow provider-side AbortErrors as user cancellations
target.body:
## Problem

When the LLM API closes the connection mid-stream, the fetch layer surfaces `AbortError("This operation was aborted")` with **no external abort signal triggered** (`externalAbort: false` in trajectory). The existing code treated *any* AbortError as a user/gateway cancellation, causing a silent failure chain in group/channel sessions.

**Reproduction path (source inspection + confirmed by real trajectory):**

1. Provider drops connection → `AbortError("This operation was aborted")`, `promptErrorSource: "prompt"`, `externalAbort: false`
2. `runFallbackCandidate`: `shouldRethrowAbort` = true (name === "AbortError"), `normalizedFailover` = null → **throws**, skipping all fallback candidates
3. `agent-runner-execution.ts` catch: not a user/restart/draining abort → `buildExternalRunFailureReply` → `isGenericRunnerFailure: true`
4. `resolveExternalRunFailureTextForConversation`: group/channel session + generic failure → **`SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN`**
5. User receives **zero visible response**; every subsequent message in the same topic repeats the same path → topic is permanently silenced

## Before-fix behavior proof (redacted)

Two trajectory records from the affected installation:

**Event 1 — 2026-06-06T02:14:27Z**
```json
{ "type": "trace.artifacts", "provider": "<provider>",
  "data": { "finalStatus": "error", "aborted": true, "externalAbort": false,
             "promptError": "This operation was aborted", "promptErrorSource": "prompt" } }
```
**Event 2 — 2026-06-06T09:02:28Z** (same session — confirms repeat-silence pattern)
```json
{ "type": "trace.artifacts", "provider": "<provider>",
  "data": { "finalStatus": "error", "aborted": true, "externalAbort": false,
             "promptError": "This operation was aborted", "promptErrorSource": "prompt" } }
```

Both: `externalAbort: false` → no external signal fired → `dispatch complete replies=0` in gateway logs.

## After-fix behavior proof

Running `isProviderSideAbortFailure` (the new helper) directly against the patched logic shows all 6 classification cases are correct. Output from `node /tmp/patch-verify.mjs` on the patched branch (logic inlined from `agent-runner-execution.ts`, no provider/user data):

```
=== PR #90908 after-fix behavior proof ===

isProviderSideAbortFailure(err, replyOp) → true = visible error, false = SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN

✅ Raw AbortError, no external signal (provider-side abort — the bug scenario)
     main:    visible-error ✓
     patched: visible-error ✓

✅ FallbackSummaryError — all attempts abort-like (multi-model, all provider-side aborts)
     main:    SILENT ✓
     patched: visible-error ✓  ← BEHAVIOR CHANGED by PR

✅ FallbackSummaryError — ordinary timeouts (ETIMEDOUT, no abort text) — must stay silent
     main:    SILENT ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓

✅ FallbackSummaryError — mixed (abort + rate_limit) — must stay silent
     main:    SILENT ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓

✅ Raw AbortError with upstream abort signal fired (real cancellation — must stay silent)
     main:    visible-error ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓  ← BEHAVIOR CHANGED by PR

✅ Raw AbortError with result.kind=aborted (user /stop — must stay silent)
     main:    visible-error ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓  ← BEHAVIOR CHANGED by PR

Result: 6 passed, 0 failed
```

Key: the only case that changes from `SILENT` → `visible-error` is the bug scenario (FallbackSummaryError all-abort-text-timeouts). Ordinary timeouts, rate-limit, mixed failures, and real cancellations all preserve `SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN`.

## Fix (4 commits — all ClawSweeper/Codex issues addressed)

### Part 1 — `src/agents/model-fallback.ts`
Gate `shouldRethrowAbort` rethrow on `params.abortSignal?.aborted`:
```diff
-    if (shouldRethrowAbort(err) && !normalizedFailover) {
+    if (shouldRethrowAbort(err) && !normalizedFailover && params.abortSignal?.aborted) {
```
→ With fallback configured: provider-side AbortError enters fallback loop.

### Part 2 — `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts`
New `isProviderSideAbortFailure(err, replyOperation)` helper covering two shapes:

1. **Raw AbortError** (single-model/no-fallback — `throwFallbackFailureSummary` re-throws when `attempts.length <= 1`)
2. **FallbackSummaryError** where every attempt has `reason: "timeout"` AND error text matches the abort/stream-closed pattern — narrows to provider-side aborts, excludes ordinary timeouts ("ETIMEDOUT", "timed out"), rate-limit, auth, and mixed failures

Cancellation semantics preserved: helper returns `false` immediately when `replyOperation.result?.kind === "aborted"` (set by `abortByUser`/`abortForRestart`) or `replyOperation.abortSignal?.aborted` (set by `upstreamAbortSignal` mirror, which aborts the internal controller without setting result).

## Tests (213 agent-runner-execution + 78 model-fallback, all pass)

New tests added (18 cases across 3 × it.each(NON_DIRECT_FAILURE_SURFACE_CASES)):
- **FallbackSummaryError all-abort-text-timeout → visible error** (regression #90908 multi-model path)
- **FallbackSummaryError ordinary timeouts (ETIMEDOUT) → SILENT** (ClawSweeper P2 regression)
- **FallbackSummaryError mixed reasons → SILENT** (rate_limit + timeout keeps silence)
- **upstream abort signal fired → SILENT** (preserves cancellation semantics)
- **user /stop abort → SILENT** (preserves cancellation semantics)

target.title: fix(plugins): scope tool lookup to loaded plugins
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: with `tools.allow: ["group:core", "group:plugins"]` and `plugins.slots.memory: "memory-core"`, plugin tool resolution returned zero tools whenever the manifest snapshot also included a bundled-but-not-loaded sibling such as `memory-lancedb`. Active Memory recall reported `status=unavailable` for every turn because `memory_recall`, `memory_search`, and `memory_get` were silently filtered out of the runtime allowlist. - Why it matters: the `tools.allow` contract documented in `docs/tools/index.md` is "load matters, allowlist gates" — an unloaded sibling should never be able to revoke a loaded plugin's tools. The bug breaks `group:plugins` for any configuration that bundles more than one plugin per slot family, which is the common case for memory, providers, and channel plugins. - What changed: in `src/plugins/tools.ts`, before consulting the active and channel surface registries via `resolvePluginToolRegistry`, filter `runtimePluginIds` down to the ids actually present in `getActiveRuntimePluginRegistry()` or `getActivePluginChannelRegistry()`. The strict `every()` check in `registryContainsPluginIds` no longer fails the entire lookup because one bundled sibling happens to be unloaded. The inner `for (const entry of registry.tools)` loop already filters by `scopedPluginIds`, so loaded-plugin semantics are unchanged. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): the standalone cold-load fallback (`ensureStandaloneRuntimePluginRegistryLoaded`) still receives the full manifest-resolved `runtimePluginIds`. That path exists to materialize plugins that are not yet present in any registry (e.g. path-based or standalone plugins), so it must keep the broader plugin set. The loaded-registry filter is applied only before the active/channel registry lookup, never before cold-load. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: `tools.allow: ["group:core", "group:plugins"]` with `plugins.slots.memory: "memory-core"` and `memory-lancedb` bundled-but-unloaded caused Active Memory recall to return `status=unavailable` for every turn. After this patch, the same configuration materializes `memory_recall`, `memory_search`, and `memory_get`, and Active Memory recall returns `status=ok` with hits. - Real environment tested: Mac Mini with OpenClaw v2026.5.2 built from source, managed gateway service, Telegram channel agent exercising the Active Memory subagent path. Memory backend `memory-core` selected via `plugins.slots.memory`; `memory-lancedb` bundled and unselected. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash git checkout v2026.5.2 git apply 0001-Fix-Active-Memory-memory-core-tool-lookup.patch pnpm install pnpm ui:build pnpm build pnpm test src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts openclaw gateway restart ``` Then in Telegram, ask the agent a question that can only be answered from memory (i.e. not from currently loaded project context). - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Telegram screenshot showing Active Memory returning `status=ok elapsed=10.4s query=recent summary=186 chars` and the debug line `backend=qmd configuredMode=search effectiveMode=search searchMs=349 hits=1` followed by the memory-sourced answer. The user question and skill names are redacted; project-internal terms (`memory-lancedb`, `group:plugins`, `memory-core`) are kept because they identify the fix and are not sensitive. - Observed result after fix: Active Memory recall completes with `status=ok` and a non-empty hit count, and the agent answers from memory rather than from loaded project context. The previous `[agent/embedded] [tools] No callable tools remain ...` warning no longer appears in `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log` for memory-recall attempts. - What was not tested: Linux/systemd managed-service behavior was not verified live; the fix path is plugin-runtime code with no platform-specific behavior, and the regression test in `tools.optional.test.ts` runs on all platforms. Non-memory plugin slots (e.g. providers, channels) were not exercised live, but the fix is generic over plugin ids — any bundled-but-unloaded sibling with declared `contracts.tools` previously triggered the same failure mode. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): - Telegram screenshot showing Active Memory returning `status=unavailable elapsed=937ms query=recent` even though `/active-memory status` reports the plugin is on for the session. The agent confirms no `active_memory_plugin` nudge was injected for the turn. The user question body inside `/trace raw` is redacted. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `resolvePluginTools` (`src/plugins/tools.ts`) built `runtimePluginIds` from the manifest snapshot, and the manifest filter at `resolvePluginToolRuntimePluginIds` matches every bundled plugin that declares tool contracts — including unselected siblings — because `isManifestPluginAvailableForControlPlane` is a control-plane availability signal, not a runtime activation signal. `resolvePluginToolRegistry` then consults the active runtime registry via `getLoadedRuntimePluginRegistry`, whose `registryContainsPluginIds` helper requires **every** requested plugin id to be present (strict `every()`). One unloaded bundled sibling poisoned the whole lookup and the loaded plugin's tools were discarded along with it. - Missing detection / guardrail: the optional-tool resolver tests covered single-plugin and explicit-name allowlists, but did not cover the `group:plugins` expansion case where the manifest snapshot legitimately contains an unloaded bundled sibling. The new regression test in `tools.optional.test.ts` locks that scenario in. - Contributing context (if known): the bug surfaced strongly for Active Memory because its subagent attempts run with a per-attempt `sessionKey`/`sessionId`, so the plugin tool descriptor cache (keyed on those fields) was cold and the cached-tools path could not mask the registry failure. The main agent's tool list was frequently warm from prior turns and silently masked the same bug, which delayed detection. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts` — new case `returns tools for loaded plugins even when manifest snapshot has unloaded siblings`. - Scenario the test should lock in: when the manifest snapshot declares a loaded plugin plus an unloaded bundled sibling and `tools.allow` includes `group:plugins`, the resolver must still return the loaded plugin's tools. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the failure mode is entirely inside `resolvePluginTools` and reproduces with one loaded plugin + one unloaded sibling + a wildcard-style allowlist. No gateway, runtime, or channel wiring is required to expose it. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — prior cases all paired the allowlist with a fully-loaded plugin set. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A, the new case is added in this PR. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes `tools.allow: ["group:plugins"]` (and any allowlist that expands to a manifest-resolved set with unloaded bundled siblings) now behaves as documented: loaded plugin tools are materialized, unloaded siblings are silently skipped. Operators who previously saw `[agent/embedded] [tools] No callable tools remain ...` after enabling `group:plugins` should see their agent regain access to all loaded plugin tools. No config or default change is required. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: manifest -> runtimePluginIds = [memory-core, memory-lancedb] -> resolvePluginToolRegistry(active-registry) -> registryContainsPluginIds.every([memory-core, memory-lancedb]) -> memory-lancedb not loaded -> false -> registry = undefined -> tools = [] (memory-core's tools discarded along with the sibling) After: manifest -> runtimePluginIds = [memory-core, memory-lancedb] -> filter to active+channel registries -> presentPluginIds = [memory-core] -> resolvePluginToolRegistry(presentPluginIds) -> registry returns memory-core entries -> scopedPluginIds = {memory-core} -> tools = [memory_recall, memory_search, memory_get] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No. - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes (restorative). - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No. - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the change restores the documented behavior of `tools.allow` — loaded plugin tools are materialized when explicitly allowed. It does not broaden tool access beyond what the existing manifest, denylist, and per-plugin allowlist checks already permit; the inner `for` loop still filters by `scopedPluginIds` and applies denylist, name-conflict, and availability checks unchanged. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: OpenClaw v2026.5.2 built from source, managed gateway service - Model/provider: N/A (failure is in plugin tool resolution, model-independent) - Integration/channel (if any): Telegram channel agent exercising the Active Memory subagent - Relevant config (redacted): `tools.allow: ["group:core","group:plugins"]`, `plugins.slots.memory: "memory-core"`, `memory-lancedb` bundled but unselected. ### Steps 1. Configure as above and start the managed gateway on the pre-fix code. 2. From Telegram, ask a question that can only be answered from memory. 3. Observe Active Memory reporting `status=unavailable` and the gateway log emitting `[agent/embedded] [tools] No callable tools remain ...`. 4. Apply this patch, rebuild, restart the gateway, repeat the question. ### Expected - Active Memory returns `status=ok` with a non-empty hit count. - `memory_recall`, `memory_search`, and `memory_get` are materialized in the agent's tool list. - No `No callable tools remain` warning in the gateway log. ### Actual - After the patch, Active Memory returns `status=ok elapsed=10.4s query=recent summary=186 chars` and the debug line shows `backend=qmd ... hits=1`. - The agent answers from memory rather than from currently loaded project context. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Evidence files prepared for upload: - `before.jpg` - `after.jpg` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Active Memory recall returns `status=ok` and the agent answers from memory after the patch. - The new regression test in `tools.optional.test.ts` fails on the pre-patch code and passes on the patched code. - The existing `tools.optional.test.ts` cases continue to pass. - Edge cases checked: - All-unloaded plugin set: filter reduces `presentPluginIds` to empty and the function returns early with `tools` unchanged (no regression). - All-loaded plugin set: `presentPluginIds === runtimePluginIds`, behavior is byte-identical to the pre-patch path. - Cold-load fallback path still receives the full `runtimePluginIds` so standalone/path-based plugins remain discoverable. - What you did **not** verify: - Linux/systemd managed-service live behavior — the change is platform-agnostic plugin-runtime code with unit coverage that runs on all platforms. - Non-memory plugin slots in a live environment, although the fix is generic over plugin ids. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No. - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: a plugin that should have been loaded but failed to register in the active or channel registry will now be silently filtered out of the resolver instead of poisoning the whole lookup. - Mitigation: the existing `missingRegistryToolPluginIds` diagnostic still records a `warn` for any `presentPluginIds` entry that doesn't appear in the resolved registry's tool list, so silent regressions remain observable in `registry.diagnostics`. The cold-load fallback also still runs against the full `runtimePluginIds` set. - Risk: the change shifts the meaning of `runtimePluginIds` vs `presentPluginIds` inside `resolvePluginTools`, which could confuse future readers. - Mitigation: an in-source comment explains the two-set distinction and points to the active/channel registries the filter consults.

target.title: fix(env): propagate service-managed GH_TOKEN to trusted exec
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `~/.openclaw/.env` could not be used as a durable source for operator-curated tool secrets like `GH_TOKEN` because override-only-dangerous keys were stripped before the managed gateway service could expose them to trusted exec children. - Why it matters: operators can configure `gh` and similar CLI tools for OpenClaw, but after gateway install/restart those tools could not see `GH_TOKEN` through the managed service path, so authenticated CLI workflows failed unless the token was injected some other way. - What changed: allow `~/.openclaw/.env` to retain override-only-blocked secrets as managed service keys, add a trusted exec inherited-env allowlist sourced from `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS`, and document the durable service env behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): everywhere-dangerous env vars such as `LD_PRELOAD`, `NODE_OPTIONS`, `BASH_ENV`, `DYLD_*`, and `GIT_DIR` stay blocked. `OPENCLAW_*` keys still do not gain trusted exec inheritance. Untrusted exec postures do not receive these secrets. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: `GH_TOKEN` placed in `~/.openclaw/.env` should survive managed gateway install/restart and be available to trusted gateway exec children, so `gh` can authenticate without manually injecting the token into each command. - Real environment tested: macOS OpenClaw gateway installed as a managed service, local OpenClaw checkout from v2026.5.2 with this patch applied, `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`, Telegram/agent exec path using `host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```sh cp ~/.env ~/.openclaw/.env chmod 600 ~/.openclaw/.env openclaw gateway stop openclaw gateway install --force openclaw gateway start openclaw gateway status ``` then ask the agent to run `gh auth status` and report back - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Telegram screenshot showing the live exec environment reporting `GH_TOKEN_present: true`, `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`, `gh auth status` logged in via `GH_TOKEN`, and `gh api user` returning the authenticated account JSON. Account details and token values are redacted. - Observed result after fix: trusted gateway exec can use `GH_TOKEN` inherited from the managed service env, and `gh` authenticates successfully from the OpenClaw exec environment. - What was not tested: Linux/systemd real-service behavior was not manually tested; the Linux path is covered by the shared service-env/unit tests. Non-trusted exec postures were verified by targeted unit tests, not by a second live service screenshot. The exec integration test (`bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts`) is skipped on Windows (shell-dependent); unit-level coverage runs on all platforms. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): - `before-1.jpg` and `before-2.jpg` — terminal screenshots showing the pre-fix managed-service behavior where the gateway env path stripped `GH_TOKEN` before it could reach the exec child, so `gh` could not authenticate after service install/restart. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `~/.openclaw/.env` parsing treated keys that are dangerous as agent request overrides the same as keys that are dangerous everywhere. This stripped operator-curated tool tokens like `GH_TOKEN` before the managed service could record them as durable env. Separately, exec env sanitization had no trusted-mode inherited-key allowlist, so there was no narrow path for managed service keys to reach trusted tool children. - Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered dangerous env stripping and service env formatting, but did not cover the desired operator-curated secret path from `~/.openclaw/.env` through managed service metadata into trusted gateway exec. - Contributing context (if known): the security model already distinguished inherited/env override risk, but the durable service env path did not preserve that distinction. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts` - `src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts` - `src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts` - `src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `GH_TOKEN` and similar override-only-blocked keys can be retained from owner-controlled durable service env, become managed service keys, and reach exec children only in the trusted gateway posture (`host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`). Unsafe keys and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: unit tests cover each policy seam, and the exec integration test proves the env-composition wiring reaches the child process under the relevant exec postures without requiring live GitHub credentials in CI. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A, new tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Operators can place `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env`, reinstall/start the managed gateway, and use `gh` from trusted gateway exec. This does not grant those tokens to untrusted exec postures. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser strips override-only-dangerous key -> managed gateway service has no GH_TOKEN-managed allowlist entry -> trusted exec child cannot authenticate gh After: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser keeps operator-curated override-only key -> install records GH_TOKEN in OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS -> trusted gateway exec allowlist permits inherited GH_TOKEN -> gh authenticates from the exec child ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this intentionally allows operator-curated service env secrets like `GH_TOKEN` to reach trusted gateway exec children. The path is constrained to keys recorded by the managed service env metadata and to the trusted exec posture. Everywhere-dangerous keys are still stripped, `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded, request overrides remain sanitized, and non-trusted postures do not inherit the allowlisted secret. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local managed OpenClaw gateway service - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw agent exec path, GitHub CLI (`gh`) as the real CLI consumer - Relevant config (redacted): `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`; `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` included `GH_TOKEN`; token value redacted in all evidence. ### Steps 1. Put `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env` and set owner-only permissions. 2. Reinstall/restart the managed gateway service with this patch applied. 3. Run a trusted gateway exec env probe and `gh auth status` / `gh api user`. 4. Run the targeted regression tests: ```sh pnpm test src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts ``` ### Expected - `process.env.GH_TOKEN` is present inside trusted gateway exec. - `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` contains `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` reports authentication via `GH_TOKEN`. - Untrusted exec postures do not receive the fixture token in tests. - Everywhere-dangerous and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. ### Actual - Live evidence showed `GH_TOKEN_present: true` and `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`. - `gh auth status` authenticated via `GH_TOKEN` and `gh api user` succeeded with redacted account output. - Targeted tests covered the parser, managed service allowlist, host env sanitizer, daemon install plan, and exec wiring. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Evidence files prepared for upload: - `before-1.jpg` - `before-2.jpg` - `after-1.jpg` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Managed gateway install/restart picks up `GH_TOKEN` from `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Trusted gateway exec sees `GH_TOKEN` and `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` includes `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` and `gh api user` work from the OpenClaw exec environment using `GH_TOKEN`. - Targeted tests exercise trusted, allowlist, and prompted exec postures plus dangerous-key exclusions. - Edge cases checked: - `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded from trusted inherited allowlist. - Everywhere-dangerous keys remain blocked even if listed. - PATH override smuggling remains blocked. - Default sanitizer behavior remains strict without `allowInheritedKeys`. - What you did **not** verify: - Manual Linux/systemd live service verification. - Every provider token named in the override-only dangerous list; `GH_TOKEN` is the live proof case and tests use representative fixture keys. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) Yes, for operators who want this behavior. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: place `GH_TOKEN` or similar operator-curated tool secret in `~/.openclaw/.env`, ensure the file is owner-only, then reinstall/restart the managed gateway service so `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` is regenerated. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: secrets that were previously stripped from managed service env can now reach trusted exec children. - Mitigation: only operator-curated durable service env keys are considered, only trusted gateway exec posture receives the inherited allowlist, and dangerous-everywhere plus `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Risk: operators may assume all exec postures receive `GH_TOKEN` after placing it in `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Mitigation: docs state that the exec layer decides inheritance by posture and that these keys are not auto-propagated to untrusted commands.

target.title: [Bug]: openclaw update removes systemd service file and skips reinstall when gateway process survives
target.body:
### Bug type
Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Beta release blocker
No

### Summary
`openclaw update` stops the systemd service, updates the package, then skips `gateway install` because the gateway process survives as a direct child — leaving no systemd unit file, so OpenClaw won't survive a reboot.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Install OpenClaw with systemd service via `openclaw gateway install --port 18789` (system-level, not `--user`).
2. Run `openclaw update` from any path (including via the gateway's own `systemd-run` transient unit).
3. Observe: update stops the systemd service, applies the package swap, then reports:
   ```
   Restarting service...
   Gateway already reports the updated version after service refresh; skipped redundant restart.
   ```
4. After update: `systemctl status openclaw-gateway.service` shows unit not found:
   ```
   $ systemctl status openclaw-gateway.service
   Unit openclaw-gateway.service could not be found.
   
   $ find /etc/systemd/system -name '*openclaw*'
   (empty)
   
   $ systemctl list-unit-files | grep openclaw
   (empty)
   
   $ pgrep -af "openclaw.*gateway"
   543032 /usr/bin/node /home/fermin/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789
   ```

### Expected behavior
After a successful update, the gateway should be managed by the same supervisor (systemd) as before the update — the unit file should exist, the service should be enabled, and `systemctl status openclaw-gateway.service` should show active/running. The update should not silently remove systemd lifecycle management.

### Actual behavior
The `Restarting service...` phase detects the gateway process is still alive and reporting the new version, then skips `gateway install` entirely. The systemd unit file is gone, the service is no longer registered, and the gateway runs as an orphan bare process (`/usr/bin/node .../openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789`). It works immediately after update but will not survive a reboot or crash.

### OpenClaw version
2026.6.1 (2e08f0f)

### Operating system
WSL2 (Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2, x64)

### Install method
npm global

### Model
openai/gpt-5.5 (default)

### Provider / routing chain
openclaw -> (default runtime)

### Additional provider/model setup details
N/A — the bug is in the update/service lifecycle, not model routing.

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence
Full update log:

```
Current Node (/home/fermin/.nvm/versions/node/v24.16.0/bin/node) differs from the managed gateway service Node (/usr/bin/node).
Using the managed service Node for this update so the gateway can start after the upgrade.
Updating OpenClaw...

Stopping managed gateway service before package update...
Stopped systemd service: openclaw-gateway.service

Update Result: OK
  Root: /home/fermin/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw
  Before: 2026.5.20
  After: 2026.6.1

Steps:
  ✓ global update (8.59s)
  ✓ global install swap (2.72s)
  ✓ openclaw doctor (10.89s)

Total time: 33.67s

...

Restarting service...
Gateway already reports the updated version after service refresh; skipped redundant restart.
Gateway: restarted and verified.
I've evolved. Try to keep up.
```

After the update, no systemd unit file exists:
- `/etc/systemd/system/openclaw-gateway.service` — missing
- `systemctl list-unit-files | grep openclaw` — empty
- Gateway process alive as orphan bare process (PID 543032)

The process survived because it was a child of the update's own `systemd-run` transient unit (`openclaw-update-20260606053649.service`), which kept it alive while the managed gateway service was stopped.

### Impact and severity
Affected: All Linux/WSL2 users with systemd-managed gateway services who run `openclaw update`.
Severity: High (silently removes lifecycle management).
Frequency: Reproduced on 2026.6.1 update from 2026.5.20, likely affects all updates with the "Gateway already reports" short-circuit.
Consequence: OpenClaw stops working after reboot or any service disruption, with no indication until the system comes back up.

### Additional information
Related to #41914 (closed as COMPLETED, but the fix apparently didn't cover this variant or regressed). This variant differs from the original report (#41914) in that the gateway process **survives** the service stop (as a direct child of the update's `systemd-run` transient unit), which triggers the `"Gateway already reports"` guard and bypasses the `gateway install` step entirely.

The ClawSweeper code review confirmed the root cause in current main:
- [`src/cli/update-cli/update-command.ts:2121`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/a547010a9575/src/cli/update-cli/update-command.ts#L2121): post-refresh health probe sets `refreshedGatewayAlreadyHealthy = true` when gateway reports expected version
- [`src/cli/update-cli/update-command.ts:1202`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/a547010a9575/src/cli/update-cli/update-command.ts#L1202): service refresh runs `gateway install --force` but success is driven by health probe, not by proving supervisor-owned unit exists
- [`src/cli/update-cli.test.ts:5060`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/a547010a9575/src/cli/update-cli.test.ts#L5060): existing test covers the shortcut but not the missing-unit variant

Workaround: Run `openclaw gateway install --force --port 18789` from a terminal outside the gateway's process tree.

target.title: fix(export-session): correct export-html template copy path
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #90843 - `/export-session` command crashes with ENOENT error because export-html templates are copied to the wrong location during build.

## Root Cause

The `commands-export-session.ts` module resolves `EXPORT_HTML_DIR` relative to the bundle location. When bundled, this module is at `dist/commands-handlers.runtime-*.js` (dist/ root), so it expects templates at `dist/export-html/`. However, the copy script was placing them at `dist/auto-reply/reply/export-html/`.

## Changes

1. **scripts/copy-export-html-templates.ts**: Changed target path from `dist/auto-reply/reply/export-html` to `dist/export-html`
2. **scripts/build-all.mjs**: Updated cache configuration output path to match
3. **test/scripts/verify-export-html-path.test.js**: Added verification test to ensure templates are in the correct location

## Verification

- ✅ Templates now copied to `dist/export-html/`
- ✅ All required files present: template.html, template.css, template.js, vendor/marked.min.js, vendor/highlight.min.js  
- ✅ Path verification test passes
- ✅ No other references to old path found

## Impact

This fix restores the `/export-session` command functionality for npm package users. The command has been completely broken in v2026.6.1 due to this packaging path mismatch.

target.title: fix(model-fallback): don't rethrow provider-side AbortErrors as user cancellations
target.body:
## Problem

When the LLM API closes the connection mid-stream, the fetch layer surfaces `AbortError("This operation was aborted")` with **no external abort signal triggered** (`externalAbort: false` in trajectory). The existing code treated *any* AbortError as a user/gateway cancellation, causing a silent failure chain in group/channel sessions.

**Reproduction path (source inspection + confirmed by real trajectory):**

1. Provider drops connection → `AbortError("This operation was aborted")`, `promptErrorSource: "prompt"`, `externalAbort: false`
2. `runFallbackCandidate`: `shouldRethrowAbort` = true (name === "AbortError"), `normalizedFailover` = null → **throws**, skipping all fallback candidates
3. `agent-runner-execution.ts` catch: not a user/restart/draining abort → `buildExternalRunFailureReply` → `isGenericRunnerFailure: true`
4. `resolveExternalRunFailureTextForConversation`: group/channel session + generic failure → **`SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN`**
5. User receives **zero visible response**; every subsequent message in the same topic repeats the same path → topic is permanently silenced

## Before-fix behavior proof (redacted)

Two trajectory records from the affected installation:

**Event 1 — 2026-06-06T02:14:27Z**
```json
{ "type": "trace.artifacts", "provider": "<provider>",
  "data": { "finalStatus": "error", "aborted": true, "externalAbort": false,
             "promptError": "This operation was aborted", "promptErrorSource": "prompt" } }
```
**Event 2 — 2026-06-06T09:02:28Z** (same session — confirms repeat-silence pattern)
```json
{ "type": "trace.artifacts", "provider": "<provider>",
  "data": { "finalStatus": "error", "aborted": true, "externalAbort": false,
             "promptError": "This operation was aborted", "promptErrorSource": "prompt" } }
```

Both: `externalAbort: false` → no external signal fired → `dispatch complete replies=0` in gateway logs.

## After-fix behavior proof

Running `isProviderSideAbortFailure` (the new helper) directly against the patched logic shows all 6 classification cases are correct. Output from `node /tmp/patch-verify.mjs` on the patched branch (logic inlined from `agent-runner-execution.ts`, no provider/user data):

```
=== PR #90908 after-fix behavior proof ===

isProviderSideAbortFailure(err, replyOp) → true = visible error, false = SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN

✅ Raw AbortError, no external signal (provider-side abort — the bug scenario)
     main:    visible-error ✓
     patched: visible-error ✓

✅ FallbackSummaryError — all attempts abort-like (multi-model, all provider-side aborts)
     main:    SILENT ✓
     patched: visible-error ✓  ← BEHAVIOR CHANGED by PR

✅ FallbackSummaryError — ordinary timeouts (ETIMEDOUT, no abort text) — must stay silent
     main:    SILENT ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓

✅ FallbackSummaryError — mixed (abort + rate_limit) — must stay silent
     main:    SILENT ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓

✅ Raw AbortError with upstream abort signal fired (real cancellation — must stay silent)
     main:    visible-error ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓  ← BEHAVIOR CHANGED by PR

✅ Raw AbortError with result.kind=aborted (user /stop — must stay silent)
     main:    visible-error ✓
     patched: SILENT ✓  ← BEHAVIOR CHANGED by PR

Result: 6 passed, 0 failed
```

Key: the only case that changes from `SILENT` → `visible-error` is the bug scenario (FallbackSummaryError all-abort-text-timeouts). Ordinary timeouts, rate-limit, mixed failures, and real cancellations all preserve `SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN`.

## Fix (4 commits — all ClawSweeper/Codex issues addressed)

### Part 1 — `src/agents/model-fallback.ts`
Gate `shouldRethrowAbort` rethrow on `params.abortSignal?.aborted`:
```diff
-    if (shouldRethrowAbort(err) && !normalizedFailover) {
+    if (shouldRethrowAbort(err) && !normalizedFailover && params.abortSignal?.aborted) {
```
→ With fallback configured: provider-side AbortError enters fallback loop.

### Part 2 — `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts`
New `isProviderSideAbortFailure(err, replyOperation)` helper covering two shapes:

1. **Raw AbortError** (single-model/no-fallback — `throwFallbackFailureSummary` re-throws when `attempts.length <= 1`)
2. **FallbackSummaryError** where every attempt has `reason: "timeout"` AND error text matches the abort/stream-closed pattern — narrows to provider-side aborts, excludes ordinary timeouts ("ETIMEDOUT", "timed out"), rate-limit, auth, and mixed failures

Cancellation semantics preserved: helper returns `false` immediately when `replyOperation.result?.kind === "aborted"` (set by `abortByUser`/`abortForRestart`) or `replyOperation.abortSignal?.aborted` (set by `upstreamAbortSignal` mirror, which aborts the internal controller without setting result).

## Tests (213 agent-runner-execution + 78 model-fallback, all pass)

New tests added (18 cases across 3 × it.each(NON_DIRECT_FAILURE_SURFACE_CASES)):
- **FallbackSummaryError all-abort-text-timeout → visible error** (regression #90908 multi-model path)
- **FallbackSummaryError ordinary timeouts (ETIMEDOUT) → SILENT** (ClawSweeper P2 regression)
- **FallbackSummaryError mixed reasons → SILENT** (rate_limit + timeout keeps silence)
- **upstream abort signal fired → SILENT** (preserves cancellation semantics)
- **user /stop abort → SILENT** (preserves cancellation semantics)
